content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

September 21, 1990


Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance & Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re:  Request For Confirmation of Telephone Advice

Dear Jeanne:

     Sometime ago, we had a telephone conversation regarding
the disclosure responsibilities of affiliated entities.  Below is
the question which I posed to you and the answer as I understood
it.

                              Question

     When a Major Donor filer (FPPC Form 461) and a Recipient
Committee filer (FPPC Form 420) are affiliated for limitation
purposes, as that term is used in FPPC Regulation 18531.5 (a) and
(c), must the campaign disclosure reports reflect combined totals?

                              Answer

     We agreed that the only relevant statute or regulation
in Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act on affiliated campaign
disclosure was FPPC Regulation 18428.  This regulation requires a
consolidated report of affiliated entities which do not receive
contributions.  This regulation is most commonly used to aggregate
contributions of parent and subsidiary corporations on a single
Major Donor campaign statement.  We both agreed that there was no
legal requirement to disclose or combine the activity of a Major
Donor filer and a Recipient Committee filer on the same campaign
statement.  You did suggest, however, that major donor filers and
recipient committees which do aggregate contributions for
limitation purposes make a footnote reference on both reports to
the fact that both entities monitor campaign contributions and do
not collectively exceed the limits provided in Chapter 5 of the
Political Reform Act.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Page 2


        Please confirm that this letter memorializes our
conversation on the subject. If you have any questions, please do
not hesitate to call me.

                                                Kind regards,


                                                Kirk Alan Pessner
                                                Paralegal/Administrator
                                                Political Reports Unit

KAP/cn

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN; MERKSAMER,
HODGSON; PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

August 20, 1990

Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Request For Advice

Dear Kathy:

This office serves as legal counsel and I serve as
treasurer to a federal nonconnected committee, San Franciscans
Getting Things Done, I.D. No. C00229039. This committee was
established on June 20, 1988. The committee was established to
permit San Francisco Mayor Art Agnos to raise and disburse funds
in support of candidates for federal office. He currently serves
as Chair of the Committee. For two years now this committee
has been raising and spending money to influence federal elections.

In calendar year 1990 and for the first time, I have been
requested to issue several contribution checks (each for less than
$1,000) to candidates for California state and local office from
this account. The total of these checks will be more than $1,000
but significantly less than $10,000. This firm has reviewed
federal campaign law and has determined that such disbursements
from this account are permissible under federal law. However, I
seek advice in permitting such disbursements from the committee in
light of the involvement of Mayor Agnos, an officeholder and
candidate as defined by the Political Reform Act.

In trying to determine what law applies to this
committee, I looked at past Commission advice.

First, you provided informal advice to the Federal
Election Commission regarding a request it received from
Congressman Don Edwards regarding his California reporting
responsibilities if his committee contributed to California state
and local candidates (FPPC Advice Letter I-89-587). In that letter
you indicated, in part, that Congressman Edwards' federal campaign
committee would not become a "committee" under California law until

Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
Page 2


it contributed $10,000 or more to California state and/or local
candidates.  In that event, it would become a "major donor
committee" as defined in Government Code 82013 (c).  That advice
letter went on to indicate that it was the Commission staff's
opinion that a federal principal campaign committee would never
become a "recipient committee" pursuant to California Government
Code section 82013(a).

     Second,  as  I  understand  it  the  Commission's
interpretation of the definition of contribution contained in
Regulation 18215(c) is that a federal separate segregated fund
(PSF) or a nonconnected federal committee is viewed identically to
a federal principal campaign committee in its first calendar year
in the eyes of the Political Reform Act (PRA), but such a committee
(PRA) would become a "recipient committee" under the PRA if, in a
second or subsequent year, the federal committee contributed $1,000
or more to California state and/or local candidates because its
donors  would have "reason to know" some of their federal
contribution was being used for California state and local
purposes.  Such a federal committee that contributes $1,000 or
more in a subsequent calendar year would then be required to
register with the Secretary of State and file Form 420 reports
disclosing not only the California disbursements/contributions to
candidates but a pro rata portion of all contributions received
by the committee that funded these disbursements.  As a "recipient
committee" under the PRA it would be subject to the transfer ban
in Government Code Section 85304 and Mayor Agnos would be a
controlling candidate and thus the committee could make no
contributions to state or local candidates.

     As mentioned above, this is the first year our committee
has considered making any contributions to California candidates.
As stated, the sum of these contributions is well below $10,000.
Mayor Art Agnos has not contributed to the committee.  We agree
that Mayor Art Agnos is a candidate as defined in Government Code
§82007;  however, this federal committee will not become a
"committee" as defined in Government Code Section 82013 in 1990
since:
a)   no contributors have made contributions to us to support
     California candidates;

b)   we will not be making any independent expenditures; and,

c)   we will not make contributions to California candidates
     totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year.

Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
Page 3


The prohibition on transfers contained in Government Code
§85304 states:
    No candidate for elective office or committee controlled
    by that candidate or candidates for elective office shall
    transfer any contribution to any other candidate for elective
    office. Transfers of funds between candidates or their controlled
    committees are prohibited.(Emphasis added)

"Controlled committee" as used in Government Code §85304 also is
specifically defined in the Act at Government Code §82016.

    It appears that San Franciscans Getting Things Done will
not be a "committee" in 1990 and therefore will not be a
"controlled committee" under the PRA. Consequently it appears to
me that this federal committee may contribute to California state
and local candidates without violating the transfer ban in
Government Code section 85304 if total PRA contributions
(candidates and ballot measures) remain below $10,000 in 1990 and
below $1,000 in any subsequent year.


    Please advise us at your earliest convenience regarding
your interpretation of this issue.

                                        Kind regards,

                                        Kirk Alan Pessner
                                        Treasurer
                                        San Franciscans Getting Things
                                        Done
